On "if thou shalt hearken diligently unto the voice of the LORD thy God... the LORD thy God will set thee on high above all nations," interpreters expound the blessing. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the covenant promise — "if thou shalt hearken diligently unto the voice of the LORD thy God, to observe and to do all his commandments... the LORD thy God will set thee on high above all nations of the earth": diligent obedience would bring exaltation above all nations; the witnesses note the significance — the blessing of the covenant on full and hearty obedience (the condition "hearken diligently" and "observe and do all," the reward exaltation above the nations); the lesson — that obedience to God is the way of blessing and honour, the people set on high; the truth that godliness is profitable, having the promise of the life that now is and of that which is to come, "godliness is profitable unto all things, having promise of the life that now is, and of that which is to come," 1 Timothy 4:8; the figure — the blessedness of the man who walks in God's law, "blessed is the man... his delight is in the law of the LORD," Psalm 1:1-2; "blessed are they that hear the word of God, and keep it," Luke 11:28; the warning that the same conditional structure brings the curse on disobedience, vv.15. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "thy sons and thy daughters shall be given unto another people, and thine eyes shall look, and fail with longing for them... and there shall be no might in thine hand" — their children would be carried off, the parents looking on helpless and pining; the thy sons and thy daughters shall be given unto another people (the children taken); the and thine eyes shall look, and fail with longing for them all the day long (the pining for them); the and there shall be no might in thine hand (the helplessness); the figure (the children carried into captivity, the parents looking on helpless, the bitterest grief; the truth that the curse would tear away their children, the parents powerless to help; the figure of the anguish of the captivity, the children lost and the hands powerless; the helpless longing for the lost children, none to deliver; the misery of the disobedient, vv.41). The verse holds together the children taken away.

On "Thy sons and thy daughters shall be given unto another people, and thine eyes shall look, and fail with longing for them all the day long," the witnesses note the grief. Gill notes the children taken and the parents pining (the anguish) — the grief (children given away).

On "and there shall be no might in thine hand," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the helplessness: "there shall be no might in thine hand" — the parents powerless to help (the helpless grief); the figure (the anguish of the captivity); the providence (the grief, the fruit and labour eaten by a strange nation to follow, vv.33). The verse displays the sons and daughters given to another people.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the grief — "thy sons and thy daughters shall be given unto another people... and there shall be no might in thine hand," the grief (Keil, Gill); "Thy sons and thy daughters shall be given unto another people, and thine eyes shall look, and fail with longing for them all the day long," the grief (Gill); "and there shall be no might in thine hand," the helplessness (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the grief. The verse marks the children: taken. The verse displays the parents: pining. The verse displays the eyes: failing. The verse displays the hand: powerless. The grief displays the anguish of the captivity. The verse displays the children: lost. The verse displays the longing: vain. The verse displays the helplessness: utter. The sons and daughters given to another people display the anguish of the captivity: thy sons and thy daughters shall be given unto another people, and thine eyes shall look, and fail with longing for them all the day long — their children carried off, the parents looking on helpless and pining with no might to help, the bitterest grief of the curse. The verse displays the fruit and labour eaten by a nation they knew not (vv.33-34).
33:
"The fruit of thy land, and all thy labours, shall a nation which thou knowest not eat up; and thou shalt be only oppressed and crushed alway:" The very brief verse notes the fruit and labour eaten by a strange nation, the people crushed. The grammatical voices note the oppression. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the fruit of thy land, and all thy labours, shall a nation which thou knowest not eat up; and thou shalt be only oppressed and crushed alway" — a foreign nation would devour their produce and labour, and they would be ground down; the the fruit of thy land, and all thy labours, shall a nation which thou knowest not eat up (the produce devoured by strangers); the and thou shalt be only oppressed and crushed alway (the continual oppression); the figure (the produce and labour devoured by a strange nation, the people crushed; the truth that the curse would give the fruit of their toil to strangers, the people ground down; the figure of the oppression that grinds the disobedient, their labour eaten by others; the continual crushing under a foreign yoke; the misery of the disobedient under the curse, vv.32). The verse holds together the produce devoured and the people crushed.

On "The fruit of thy land, and all thy labours, shall a nation which thou knowest not eat up," the witnesses note the devouring. Gill notes the produce devoured by a strange nation (the oppression) — the devouring (by strangers).

On "and thou shalt be only oppressed and crushed alway," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the crushing: "thou shalt be only oppressed and crushed alway" — the people ground down continually (the oppression); the figure (the foreign yoke that crushes); the providence (the oppression, the madness at the sights they see to follow, vv.34). The verse displays the fruit and labour eaten by a strange nation.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the oppression — "the fruit of thy land, and all thy labours, shall a nation which thou knowest not eat up; and thou shalt be only oppressed and crushed alway," the oppression (Keil, Gill); "The fruit of thy land, and all thy labours, shall a nation which thou knowest not eat up," the devouring (Gill); "and thou shalt be only oppressed and crushed alway," the crushing (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the oppression. The verse marks the produce: devoured. The verse displays the labour: eaten. The verse displays the people: crushed. The verse displays the yoke: foreign. The oppression displays the curse that grinds the disobedient. The verse displays the strangers: devouring. The verse displays the people: oppressed. The verse displays the crushing: continual. The fruit and labour eaten by a strange nation display the curse that grinds the disobedient: the fruit of thy land, and all thy labours, shall a nation which thou knowest not eat up; and thou shalt be only oppressed and crushed alway — a foreign nation devouring their produce and labour, the people ground down continually under the foreign yoke, the misery of the disobedient under the curse. The verse displays the madness at the sights their eyes would see (v.34).
34:
"So that thou shalt be mad for the sight of thine eyes which thou shalt see." The very brief verse notes the madness at the sights of misery they would see. The grammatical voices note the madness. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "thou shalt be mad for the sight of thine eyes which thou shalt see" — the sights of misery and calamity would drive them mad; the so that thou shalt be mad (driven to madness); the for the sight of thine eyes which thou shalt see (at the sights of misery); the figure (the madness that the sights of calamity bring, the mind overthrown by misery; the truth that the accumulated miseries would derange the mind, madness from grief; the figure of the despair that sin's judgments breed, the mind broken by what the eyes behold; the madness at the spectacle of ruin, the reason overthrown; the depth of the misery, vv.28). The verse closes this section of the curses.

On "So that thou shalt be mad," the witnesses note the madness. Gill notes the madness from misery (the despair) — the madness (driven to it).

On "for the sight of thine eyes which thou shalt see," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the sights: "for the sight of thine eyes which thou shalt see" — the madness at the sights of calamity (the mind overthrown); the figure (the despair that sin's judgments breed); the providence (the madness, the further curses on body and exile to follow, vv.35). The verse displays the madness at the sights of misery they would see.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the madness — "thou shalt be mad for the sight of thine eyes which thou shalt see," the madness (Keil, Gill); "So that thou shalt be mad," the madness (Gill); "for the sight of thine eyes which thou shalt see," the sights (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the madness. The verse marks the mind: overthrown. The verse displays the sights: of misery. The verse displays the despair: bred. The verse displays the reason: broken. The madness displays the despair that sin's judgments breed. The verse displays the misery: beheld. The verse displays the mind: deranged. The verse displays the calamity: maddening. The madness at the sights of misery they would see displays the despair that sin's judgments breed: thou shalt be mad for the sight of thine eyes which thou shalt see — the sights of misery and calamity driving them mad, the mind overthrown by what the eyes behold, the depth of the misery under the curse. The verse closes this section, before the further curses on body, exile, and ruin (vv.35-68).
35:
On Deuteronomy 28:35 — “The LORD shall smite thee in the knees, and in the legs, with a sore botch that cannot be healed, from the sole of thy foot unto the top of thy head” — the witnesses mark the incurable disease threatened.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: grievous abscesses in the knees and thighs, incurable, from the sole of the foot to the crown — the so-called joint-leprosy. Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott: a sore botch, an inflammatory burning boil (Deuteronomy 28:27). The Reformed Gill: painful in those parts and not easily cured, incurable by the art of man. Calvin: all the posterity of Adam, infected with sin, is liable to maladies.

The verse threatens disease. An incurable boil smites them from foot to head. The body itself is cursed with sickness.

So the witnesses see the curse of an incurable disease, covering the body from sole to crown, threatened upon disobedience. The witnesses note the affliction: “a sore botch that cannot be healed” — Gill and Keil describe it as a grievous, burning boil or abscess, settling in the knees and legs but spreading over the whole body, from the sole of the foot to the top of the head; a disease so painful and so deep that it was incurable by the art of man, the “joint-leprosy” or some like plague. They mark the totality of it: from foot to head, no part left whole; the whole man smitten with sickness, as Job was (Job 2:7). They observe Calvin’s sober reflection: that since death is common to all the race of Adam, all are subject to disease, the whole posterity of Adam being infected with the taint of sin and so liable to maladies; the curse of sickness lies on fallen man, and is here threatened in its sharpest form on the disobedient. They note the dread the witnesses see in an incurable plague: that of all afflictions, the disease that no remedy can touch is among the most terrible, leaving the sufferer without hope of relief; God’s hand, when it smites in judgment, can lay on the body a stroke past all healing. They observe that this is but one of the long catalogue of curses, each more dreadful than the last. They note that the body, made for God, is in judgment given over to corruption. The verse thus threatens the incurable disease — the captivity of the king following in the next verse.
36:
On Deuteronomy 28:36 — “The LORD shall bring thee, and thy king which thou shalt set over thee, unto a nation which neither thou nor thy fathers have known; and there shalt thou serve other gods, wood and stone” — the witnesses mark the king carried into captivity.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: the loss of their independence; the king and people carried to a nation unknown. Charles Ellicott: Moses foreknew Israel would have a king (Deuteronomy 17:14). The Reformed Gill: fulfilled in Jehoiachin and Zedekiah, carried captive to Babylon. Calvin: the fulfilment taught the Jews, too late, it was no empty threat. Wesley: the calamity universal, which even the king could not avoid.

The verse foretells exile. King and people are carried to an unknown nation, to serve idols. The whole nation, even its king, goes into captivity.

So the witnesses see the curse rise to the carrying away of the whole nation, king and all, into a strange land. The witnesses note the captivity: “the LORD shall bring thee, and thy king… unto a nation which neither thou nor thy fathers have known” — Gill marks its fulfilment in Jehoiachin and Zedekiah, kings of Judah, carried captive to Babylon by Nebuchadnezzar (2 Kings 24:15; 2 Kings 25:7); the king himself, who should have protected the people, was led away with them. They mark Wesley’s point on the universality of the blow: that the calamity would be so complete that even the king could not escape it, much less the common people who had far less means of flight; and so irrecoverable, since he who should have been their defence was himself a captive. They observe Ellicott’s note on the prophecy: that here, as at Deuteronomy 17:14, Moses shows his foreknowledge that Israel would one day have a king — a thing no later writer, he asks, could have concealed or invented; the prophecy is genuine and far-reaching. They mark the bitterest part: “there shalt thou serve other gods, wood and stone” — in their exile they would be forced, or drawn, to serve the idols of their captors; the people who would not serve the living God should serve dead wood and stone in a strange land. They observe Calvin’s solemn word: that the fulfilment of this prophecy taught the Jews, though too late, that it was no empty threat to frighten them, but the sure word of God. They note that the loss of king, land, and worship was the height of the curse. The verse thus foretells the exile — the byword among nations following in the next verse.
37:
On Deuteronomy 28:37 — “And thou shalt become an astonishment, a proverb, and a byword, among all nations whither the LORD shall lead thee” — the witnesses mark Israel made a byword among the nations.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: an astonishment, a proverb, and a byword among all nations. Charles Ellicott: the contrary of Deuteronomy 28:10; verified in the first captivity (1 Kings 9:7). The Reformed Gill: an astonishment to neighbouring nations that hear of their overthrow. Calvin: the climax of misery, that they should meet on every side with taunts and insults.

The verse adds reproach. They become an astonishment and a byword among the nations. To their ruin is added scorn.

So the witnesses see the curse heightened by the scorn of the nations, Israel made a proverb and a byword. The witnesses note the shame: “thou shalt become an astonishment, a proverb, and a byword” — Gill observes they would be an astonishment to the nations that heard of their overthrow and saw the misery they were brought into; a proverb and a byword, their very name become a token of disaster and a jest in the mouths of men. They mark Ellicott’s observation that this is the exact contrary of the blessing of Deuteronomy 28:10, that all the peoples should see them called by the name of the LORD and fear them; instead, they should be the byword of the peoples; and that this was verified already in the first captivity, as Solomon had foretold of the ruined temple and nation (1 Kings 9:7). They observe Calvin’s point that this is the very climax of their miseries: that they should be so far from finding any consolation from men, that on every side their misery should be met with taunts and insults; for nothing more bitterly wounds the wretched than to be mocked in their affliction. They note the strange and lasting fulfilment the witnesses see: that the name of the Jew did indeed become, through the ages of their dispersion, a byword and a reproach among the nations, their fall a proverb. They observe that to be despised in ruin is the sharpest stroke of the curse. They note that the people who would not glorify God were made a spectacle of shame. The verse thus adds the reproach — the curse on the land’s produce following in the next verses.
38:
On Deuteronomy 28:38 — “Thou shalt carry much seed out into the field, and shalt gather but little in; for the locust shall consume it” — the witnesses mark the curse of fruitless labour.

The Reformed Gill: this refers to them while in their own land, before the captivity. Calvin: the harvest shall be scanty, notwithstanding an abundant sowing, because the locust shall devour it.

The verse curses the harvest. Much sown, little reaped, the locust devouring. Their labour is given to the destroyer.

So the witnesses see the curse fall on the fruit of their labour, much sown but little reaped, the locust consuming the increase. The witnesses note the disappointment: “thou shalt carry much seed out into the field, and shalt gather but little in” — Gill observes that this and the verses following refer to the people while yet in their own land, before the captivity; they would sow plentifully, in expectation of a full harvest, but reap only a little. They mark the cause: “for the locust shall consume it” — Calvin draws out that the harvest would be scanty notwithstanding the abundant sowing, because the locust would devour the crop; the increase that should have come from their toil would be eaten up by the destroyer before they could gather it. They observe the bitterness of this kind of curse, which the witnesses feel: that to labour and sow, and then to see the fruit devoured and reap almost nothing, is a peculiar torment — the disappointment of hope, the waste of toil, the watching of one’s substance perish before one’s eyes. They note the reversal it works: that the very blessing of the land, its fruitfulness, is turned to a curse, the seed swallowed and the harvest lost; what God gives in mercy he withholds, by such plagues, in judgment. They observe the lesson the witnesses draw: that the increase of the field is in God’s hand, and that he can blast the labour of the disobedient, that they reap not what they sow. They note that the locust comes at God’s bidding to undo man’s work. The verse thus curses the harvest — the vineyard following in the next verse.
39:
On Deuteronomy 28:39 — “Thou shalt plant vineyards, and dress them, but shalt neither drink of the wine, nor gather the grapes; for the worms shall eat them” — the witnesses mark the curse on the vineyard.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: thou shalt plant and dress vineyards, but not drink the wine nor gather the grapes, for the worms shall eat them. The Reformed Gill: plant and prune them, in expectation of much fruit.

The verse curses the vineyard. They plant and dress, but the worm devours the grapes. Their care brings no fruit to them.

So the witnesses see the curse extend to the vineyard, that the people should plant and tend it yet drink none of its wine. The witnesses note the labour and its loss: “thou shalt plant vineyards, and dress them, but shalt neither drink of the wine, nor gather the grapes” — Gill observes that they would plant the vines and prune them, in expectation of much fruit, bestowing all the care and toil that a vineyard requires; yet they should neither gather the grapes nor drink the wine. They mark the cause: “for the worms shall eat them” — the worm or grub would devour the fruit and the vine, so that all their dressing and tending should come to nothing; the labour of the husbandman should profit another, or rather no one, the increase being eaten by the destroyer. They observe the kind of curse the witnesses see here, like that on the corn: that it is the curse of frustrated labour and disappointed hope — to do all the work and lose all the reward, to plant and not to drink; a sore vexation, and a token of God’s displeasure, that the toil of his people should be in vain. They note the contrast with the blessing the witnesses recall: that in mercy God gave his people to plant vineyards and eat the fruit (Deuteronomy 6:11), but in judgment he gives them to plant and not to gather; the same vine that should have gladdened them is made a grief. They observe that God can turn the gladdest gift to bitterness when his people forsake him. They note that the worm comes at his word. The verse thus curses the vineyard — the olive following in the next verse.
40:
On Deuteronomy 28:40 — “Thou shalt have olive trees throughout all thy coasts, but thou shalt not anoint thyself with the oil; for thine olive shall cast his fruit” — the witnesses mark the curse on the olive.

The Reformed Gill: olive trees throughout the land of Canaan, a land of olive oil; yet they should not anoint themselves, for the olive should cast its fruit.

The verse curses the olive. They have olive trees, but no oil, for the fruit falls. The land of oil yields them none.

So the witnesses see the curse touch the olive also, that the people should have the trees yet have no oil. The witnesses note the seeming plenty and the real want: “thou shalt have olive trees throughout all thy coasts, but thou shalt not anoint thyself with the oil” — Gill observes that the land of Canaan was a land of olive oil, abounding in olive trees in every part; yet the people should not have the use of the oil, though the trees stood in all their borders. They mark the cause: “for thine olive shall cast his fruit” — the olive would drop its fruit before it ripened, the unripe olives falling and perishing, so that no oil could be pressed; the tree should stand, but yield nothing for use. They observe the bitterness the witnesses see in this kind of curse, like the foregoing: that the people should have the appearance of plenty — the field sown, the vineyard planted, the olive trees standing — and yet the substance of want; the trees in their coasts mocking them with a fruitfulness that came to nothing. They note the use of the oil that they should lose: the anointing of the body, a token of gladness, health, and refreshment; the want of it a mark of mourning and of poverty. They observe the principle the witnesses draw throughout these verses: that God can give the means of plenty and yet withhold its enjoyment, blasting the increase at the last, that his people, in the midst of seeming abundance, should suffer real lack. They note that the curse follows the disobedient into every part of their labour. The verse thus curses the olive — the captivity of the children following in the next verse.
41:
On Deuteronomy 28:41 — “Thou shalt beget sons and daughters, but thou shalt not enjoy them; for they shall go into captivity” — the witnesses mark the children lost to captivity.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: sons and daughters they would beget, but not keep, because they would go into captivity. The Reformed Gill: “they shall not be thine,” being taken and given to others.

The verse curses their offspring. They beget children but lose them to captivity. Even their family is taken from them.

So the witnesses see the curse reach the dearest things, the people’s own children, lost to them by captivity. The witnesses note the bitter loss: “thou shalt beget sons and daughters, but thou shalt not enjoy them” — Gill renders it “they shall not be thine,” the children being taken from the parents and given to others; the joy and comfort of offspring should be denied them. They mark the cause: “for they shall go into captivity” — Keil observes the parents would beget sons and daughters but not keep them, because they would be carried away into captivity by the enemy; the children born to them should be torn from their arms and led into a strange land. They observe the depth of this curse the witnesses feel: that of all losses, the loss of one’s children is among the most grievous; to bear and rear them, and then to see them carried off into slavery, is a sorrow that pierces the heart; the curse strikes not only at their goods but at their dearest affections. They note the reversal it works: that children, the blessing and the heritage of the LORD (Psalm 127:3), should become a grief, begotten only to be lost; the very fruitfulness of the family turned to mourning. They observe that the disobedient should be stripped of all — field, vine, olive, and now their own offspring; nothing left them to enjoy. They note that God can take from his people the comforts they hold dearest. The verse thus curses their offspring — the locust consuming all in the next verse.
42:
On Deuteronomy 28:42 — “All thy trees and fruit of thy land shall the locust consume” — the witnesses mark the locust devouring all.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: tselatsal, from a root “to buzz,” a rhetorical epithet for the locust. The Reformed Gill: a creature that consumes not only grass and corn but green trees.

The verse sums the locust’s ruin. All trees and fruit are consumed by the locust. The destroyer takes the whole increase.

So the witnesses see the curse on the produce summed in the locust’s consuming of all the trees and fruit of the land. The witnesses note the devourer: “all thy trees and fruit of thy land shall the locust consume” — Gill observes that the locust is a creature that consumes not only the grass and herbs and corn of the field, but the green trees also, stripping them bare; nothing of the land’s increase should escape it. They mark the word Keil notes, tselatsal, from a root meaning “to buzz,” a vivid epithet for the locust, naming it by the whirring of its wings; the very sound of the swarm a token of the coming ruin. They observe the totality the witnesses see in this verse, gathering up the foregoing: that field and vine and olive and tree, all the produce of the land, should be given over to the locust; the destroyer should leave nothing, the whole increase swallowed. They note the helplessness of man before such a plague: that against the locust, coming in its clouds, no labour or care avails; the husbandman can but watch his year’s work devoured in a day. They observe the lesson the witnesses draw: that the smallest of creatures, at God’s command, can undo the labour of a nation, and that the disobedient are at the mercy of the meanest of God’s instruments of judgment (Joel 1:4). They note that God arms the locust against his rebellious people. They observe that the curse on the land was complete. The verse thus sums the locust’s ruin — the rising of the stranger following in the next verse.
43:
On Deuteronomy 28:43 — “The stranger that is within thee shall get up above thee very high; and thou shalt come down very low” — the witnesses mark the reversal of rank between native and stranger.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: Israel utterly impoverished, sinking lower, while the stranger rose higher. The Reformed Gill: the stranger above in wealth, power, honour. Calvin: a mark of God’s wrath, that the sojourners should become masters. Wesley: the stranger within thy gates, who formerly served thee and were glad of thy crumbs.

The verse reverses the order. The stranger rises high, the native sinks low. Those once beneath them are set above.

So the witnesses see the curse work a bitter reversal, the stranger rising above the native Israelite who sinks low. The witnesses note the exaltation of the stranger: “the stranger that is within thee shall get up above thee very high” — Gill marks his rising in wealth, power, authority, and honour; Wesley adds that this was the stranger within their gates, who formerly served them and were glad of the crumbs that fell from their table, now lifted above them. They mark the sinking of the native: “and thou shalt come down very low” — Keil observes that Israel would be utterly impoverished, and sink lower and lower, while the stranger in their midst rose higher and higher; the order of things wholly overturned. They observe Calvin’s point that this was no doubtful mark of God’s wrath: that the sojourners, who dwelt in the land of Canaan only by sufferance, should in a manner become its masters; as the debtor falls under the power of his creditor, so Israel, impoverished and in debt, should be subject to the stranger they had once received. They note the shame the witnesses feel in this: that the chosen people, set high among the nations in the day of their obedience, should in their fall be made lowest of all, beneath the very strangers in their gates; the humbling was complete. They observe the contrast with the blessing the witnesses recall: that God had promised, in obedience, to set them on high above all nations (Deuteronomy 28:1); in disobedience, the very stranger is set above them. They note that pride and rebellion are repaid with abasement. The verse thus reverses the order — the lending and borrowing following in the next verse.
44:
On Deuteronomy 28:44 — “He shall lend to thee, and thou shalt not lend to him: he shall be the head, and thou shalt be the tail” — the witnesses mark the stranger become the head.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: the opposite of Deuteronomy 28:12–13 would come to pass. The Reformed Gill: the stranger able to lend to the Jew, the Jew not able to lend to him, his circumstances being so reduced.

The verse completes the reversal. The stranger lends and is the head; the Jew borrows and is the tail. The blessing is turned upside down.

So the witnesses see the reversal completed: the stranger become the lender and the head, the Israelite the borrower and the tail. The witnesses note the exchange: “he shall lend to thee, and thou shalt not lend to him” — Gill observes that the stranger should be in a position to lend to the Jew, while the Jew, his circumstances so reduced, should not be able to lend to the stranger; the one rich and the other poor, the one a creditor and the other a debtor. They mark the figure: “he shall be the head, and thou shalt be the tail” — the stranger should hold the chief place, ruling and leading, and the Israelite the lowest, following and serving; the head and the tail naming the highest and the meanest. They observe the express point Keil makes, that this is the exact opposite of the blessing promised in obedience: that Israel should lend to many nations and not borrow, and should be the head and not the tail (Deuteronomy 28:12–13); the curse turns the very words of the blessing upside down. They note the completeness of the abasement the witnesses see: that the people who, in obedience, should have led the nations and lent to them, in disobedience should be led and should borrow, beneath the stranger in their own land. They observe the lesson the witnesses draw: that the blessings of God are held on the condition of obedience, and that the same God who would have exalted his people exactly reverses their state when they forsake him; the head becomes the tail. They note that to refuse God’s service is to fall under the service of men. The verse thus completes the reversal — the curses summed as pursuing them in the next verse.
45:
On Deuteronomy 28:45 — “Moreover all these curses shall come upon thee, and shall pursue thee, and overtake thee, till thou be destroyed; because thou hearkenedst not unto the voice of the LORD thy God” — the witnesses mark the curses pursuing to destruction.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: “till thou be destroyed” — not exterminated, but crushed (2 Kings 13:7). The Puritan Henry: to show how deep the treasures of that wrath are. The Reformed Calvin: he takes away all hope of alleviation, the scourges not ceasing till they repent. Wesley: hitherto Moses foretold the captivity in Babylon; in the following, the later dispersion.

The verse presses the curses home. They shall pursue and overtake till destruction, for disobedience. The wrath does not cease.

So the witnesses see the curses gathered and pressed home: they shall pursue, overtake, and destroy the disobedient. The witnesses note the relentlessness: “all these curses shall come upon thee, and shall pursue thee, and overtake thee, till thou be destroyed” — Henry observes that, though enough had been said to fill them with dread, here is shown how deep the treasures of that wrath are; the curses are personified as hunters that chase the fugitive, run him down, and overtake him, leaving no escape. They mark Ellicott’s note on “destroyed”: that the word means not utterly exterminated, but crushed and broken, ground down (2 Kings 13:7); the nation should be shattered, though a remnant remain. They observe Calvin’s point that here Moses takes away all hope of alleviation: that the scourges of God should not cease until the people repented; the curses would follow them without intermission so long as their disobedience lasted. They mark the cause restated: “because thou hearkenedst not unto the voice of the LORD thy God” — all this came not by chance nor by mere misfortune, but as the just recompense of their refusing to obey God. They note the structural observation Wesley makes: that hitherto Moses had been chiefly foretelling the captivity in Babylon, by which even after their return they were brought low; but in the verses following he foretells the later and more dreadful dispersion under the Romans. They observe that the wrath of God, once kindled against persistent sin, is unwearied in its pursuit. They note that disobedience is the root of all the woe. The verse thus presses the curses home — their standing as a sign following in the next verse.
46:
On Deuteronomy 28:46 — “And they shall be upon thee for a sign and for a wonder, and upon thy seed for ever” — the witnesses mark the curses a lasting sign and wonder.

The Reformed Gill: the curses should rest and remain upon them, visible, observed by all. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: a wonder, signal to all who hear — amazing that a people so incorporated should be so universally dispersed, yet scattered in all nations should not mingle and be lost.

The verse marks the wonder. The curses are a sign and a wonder on them and their seed forever. Their state itself is a marvel.

So the witnesses see the curses themselves become a lasting sign and wonder, upon the people and their seed forever. The witnesses note the abiding mark: “they shall be upon thee for a sign and for a wonder… for ever” — Gill observes that the curses should rest and remain upon them, visible and continuing, so plainly that all who saw should observe and be amazed; their very condition a sign to the nations. They mark the wonder Wesley draws out at length, as a thing amazing in the history of the world: that a people so closely incorporated, so distinct a nation, should be so universally dispersed among all peoples; and yet that a people scattered into all the nations of the earth should not, as every other scattered people has, mingle with the nations and be lost, but remain through all the ages a separate and recognizable people, the standing monument of this prophecy. They observe the force the witnesses see in this: that the preservation of the Jews in their dispersion, distinct and unabsorbed, is itself a continuing sign and wonder, an open proof of the truth of God’s word, both in the curse fulfilled and in the people preserved for the future mercy (Romans 11:25). They note that the very existence of the scattered Jew, neither destroyed nor assimilated, is a marvel that no natural cause explains. They observe that God has made his judgment on them a perpetual testimony to all the nations. They note that the sign is upon their seed, generation after generation. The verse thus marks the wonder — the cause in their ingratitude following in the next verse.
47:
On Deuteronomy 28:47 — “Because thou servedst not the LORD thy God with joyfulness, and with gladness of heart, for the abundance of all things” — the witnesses mark the sin of unthankful service.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: because it had not served the LORD with joy and gladness of heart, for the abundance of all the blessings bestowed on it. The Reformed Gill: by attending his worship and keeping his commandments.

The verse names the sin. They served not God with joy in their abundance. Unthankful service brings the curse.

So the witnesses see the deep root of the curse named: that the people had not served God with joy and gladness in the day of their abundance. The witnesses note the charge: “because thou servedst not the LORD thy God with joyfulness, and with gladness of heart” — Gill observes the failure was both in not attending God’s worship and keeping his commandments, and in not doing so with a willing and joyful heart; the service God requires is not grudging or cold, but cheerful and glad. They mark the aggravation Keil draws out: “for the abundance of all things” — that they had failed to serve God joyfully in the very time when he had loaded them with all blessings; their ingratitude was the deeper because their mercies were the greater; they had abundance of all things from God’s hand, and yet did not serve him gladly. They observe the searching truth the witnesses see in this: that God takes account not only of whether we serve him, but of how — whether with joy and thankfulness or with reluctance and complaint; and that to enjoy his abundant gifts without serving him gladly is a great sin, the basest ingratitude. They note the justice the next verse will draw out: that because they would not serve God with joy in plenty, they should serve their enemies in hunger and want; the punishment answers the sin. They observe that joyful, thankful service is the duty of those whom God has blessed. They note that ingratitude in plenty provokes God to send want. The verse thus names the sin — the servitude that answers it following in the next verse.
48:
On Deuteronomy 28:48 — “Therefore shalt thou serve thine enemies… in hunger, and in thirst, and in nakedness, and in want of all things: and he shall put a yoke of iron upon thy neck, until he have destroyed thee” — the witnesses mark the servitude to enemies, the answer to ingratitude.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: it would serve its enemies in hunger and want, with a yoke of iron. Adam Clarke: because they would not serve God, they became slaves to men. The Reformed Gill: since they would not serve the Lord, so good a master.

The verse gives the just answer. They serve their enemies in want, under a yoke of iron. The refusal of God’s service brings bondage to men.

So the witnesses see the just recompense of their ingratitude: that they should serve their enemies in want, who would not serve God in plenty. The witnesses note the exchange: “therefore shalt thou serve thine enemies… in hunger, and in thirst, and in nakedness, and in want of all things” — Gill draws out the fit answer: since they would not serve the LORD their God, so good a master, who supplied them with all good things and with plenty, they should be made to serve cruel enemies in the utter lack of all things; the want they suffer the exact opposite of the abundance they ungratefully enjoyed. They mark Clarke’s pointed observation: that because they would not serve God, they became slaves to men; the refusal of the willing, joyful service of God issues in the hard, compelled service of enemies. They observe the harshness of the bondage: “he shall put a yoke of iron upon thy neck, until he have destroyed thee” — a yoke of iron, the figure of a servitude heavy, galling, and inescapable, pressing them down until they were crushed. They note the lesson the witnesses see: that there is no escaping service; the only choice is whom we will serve — God, whose service is freedom and joy, or sin and enemies, whose service is bondage and want; those who cast off the easy yoke of God take on the iron yoke of their foes. They observe that the punishment answers the sin with exact justice, want for plenty, slavery for refused service. They note that the service of God is the truest liberty. The verse thus gives the just answer — the nation from far described in the next verses.
49:
On Deuteronomy 28:49 — “The LORD shall bring a nation against thee from far, from the end of the earth, as swift as the eagle flieth; a nation whose tongue thou shalt not understand” — the witnesses mark the nation from afar, with a disputed identity.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: probably the Romans — the eagle their standard, the Latin tongue not understood. Charles Ellicott: compare Jeremiah 5:15, “a nation whose language thou knowest not” (the Chaldeans). The Reformed Gill: Babylon was “from far,” but not “from the end of the earth”; so rather the Romans. Calvin: unknown and barbarous enemies, attacking with impetuosity.

The verse foretells the invader. A swift, distant nation of unknown tongue comes against them. The eagle-nation descends in judgment.

So the witnesses see the curse rise to a foreign invasion, a nation from the end of the earth swooping like the eagle. The witnesses note the description: “a nation against thee from far, from the end of the earth, as swift as the eagle flieth; a nation whose tongue thou shalt not understand” — a distant, swift, and strange people, coming with the speed of the eagle upon its prey, speaking a tongue the people could not understand. They mark the dispute among the witnesses over whom this primarily names. Clarke takes it of the Romans: the eagle was the very emblem on the Roman standards, the Roman eagle proverbial; and the Latin was a tongue the Jews knew not. Gill argues the same way: that Babylon, though “from far,” was not “from the end of the earth,” and so the description fits the Romans better. But Ellicott connects it rather with the Chaldeans, citing the like prophecy of Jeremiah, “Lo, I will bring a nation upon you from far… a nation whose language thou knowest not” (Jeremiah 5:15), spoken of Babylon. They observe that the words, in their fulness, may take in both — the Chaldean invasion under Nebuchadnezzar and the Roman under Titus — the prophecy reaching to more than one judgment. They note Calvin’s general sense: that unknown and barbarous enemies should come, attacking with great impetuosity and violence. They observe that the eagle’s swiftness marks the suddenness and the irresistibleness of the stroke. They note that the foreign tongue marks the strangeness and terror of the invader. The verse thus foretells the invader — his fierceness described in the next verse.
50:
On Deuteronomy 28:50 — “A nation of fierce countenance, which shall not regard the person of the old, nor shew favour to the young” — the witnesses mark the merciless invader.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: a nation of fierce countenance, regarding neither old nor young. Charles Ellicott: compare 2 Chronicles 36:17 — the king of the Chaldees had no compassion on young or old. The Reformed Gill: “strong of face,” aptly describing the old Romans, fierce and terrible.

The verse marks the invader’s cruelty. A fierce nation spares neither old nor young. No mercy is shown to any.

So the witnesses see the invader marked by a pitiless cruelty, sparing neither age nor youth. The witnesses note the fierceness: “a nation of fierce countenance” — Gill renders it “strong of face,” a bold, hard, terrible people, fearing none; he applies it aptly to the old Romans, always represented as fierce and unyielding, as the Chaldeans too were a nation dreadful and terrible (Habakkuk 1:7). They mark the mercilessness: “which shall not regard the person of the old, nor shew favour to the young” — the conqueror should show no respect to the aged, whom common humanity reverences, nor pity to the young and helpless, whom nature spares; Ellicott cites the like word of the Chaldeans, who “had no compassion upon young man or maiden, old man or him that stooped for age” (2 Chronicles 36:17). They observe the horror the witnesses feel in this: that the invader should be utterly without compassion, slaughtering the gray-headed and the children alike; the war waged with a savagery that knew no bounds of pity. They note the fitness of the description to both the great destructions the witnesses have in view, the Babylonian and the Roman, in each of which the aged and the young perished without mercy. They observe that God’s judgment, when it comes by the hand of a cruel enemy, spares none; the rod is fierce because the sin was great. They note that those who refused the mercy of God fell into the hands of the merciless. The verse thus marks the invader’s cruelty — his devouring of the land’s produce following in the next verses.
51:
On Deuteronomy 28:51 — “And he shall eat the fruit of thy cattle, and the fruit of thy land, until thou be destroyed… he shall not leave thee either corn, wine, or oil” — the witnesses mark the invader consuming all.

The Reformed Gill: he shall eat the fruit of thy cattle, larger and lesser, oxen and sheep, their calves and lambs and kids.

The verse strips the land. The invader devours cattle and crops, leaving nothing. The enemy eats up all their substance.

So the witnesses see the invader strip the land bare, devouring the cattle and the produce until nothing is left. The witnesses note the consuming: “he shall eat the fruit of thy cattle, and the fruit of thy land” — Gill observes that the enemy should eat up the increase of their herds and flocks, larger and lesser, the calves and lambs and kids, and the fruit of the ground as well; all that the land and the cattle yielded should go to feed the conqueror. They mark the totality: “until thou be destroyed… he shall not leave thee either corn, wine, or oil, or the increase of thy kine, or flocks of thy sheep” — the enemy should leave them nothing, neither the staples of bread, wine, and oil, nor the young of their cattle and sheep; the land should be eaten down to bareness and the people brought to destruction by want. They observe the picture the witnesses see: of an invading army living off the land, consuming in its march all the substance of the people, so that famine followed the sword; the conqueror’s consuming and the people’s starving were one. They note the contrast with the curse of the locust earlier: that there the insect devoured the increase, here the enemy; by both, in judgment, the people are stripped of all and left to perish. They observe that war and famine come together as twin scourges of God’s wrath. They note that the disobedient should be eaten out of house and land. They observe that the enemy was God’s rod to consume them. The verse thus strips the land — the siege of their cities following in the next verse.
52:
On Deuteronomy 28:52 — “And he shall besiege thee in all thy gates, until thy high and fenced walls come down, wherein thou trustedst” — the witnesses mark the besieging of their strongholds.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: Nebuchadnezzar first (2 Kings 25:1), and Titus next; he shall beset thee round, casting a trench, as our Lord predicted (Luke 19:43). Charles Ellicott: the sieges by Nebuchadnezzar (Jeremiah 34:7) and the Romans. The Reformed Gill: in all their cities and walled towns. Calvin: he overthrows their false confidence in their fortresses.

The verse foretells the siege. The enemy besieges all their cities till the high walls fall. Their trusted strongholds cannot save them.

So the witnesses see the invader lay siege to all the fortified cities, until the walls in which they trusted came down. The witnesses note the universal siege: “he shall besiege thee in all thy gates” — Gill observes this means in all their cities and walled towns, which had gates and bars for defence; the enemy should beset every stronghold throughout the land. They mark the fall of their defences: “until thy high and fenced walls come down, wherein thou trustedst” — the very walls they relied on for safety should be thrown down; Calvin draws out that God overthrows their false confidence, for the number and strength of their fortified towns had inspired them with a vain courage, as if no enemy could persist long enough to take them all. They observe the historical fulfilments the witnesses point to: Clarke names Nebuchadnezzar first, who besieged and took Jerusalem (2 Kings 25:1), and Titus next, the Roman; and he notes that the enemy should “beset thee round on every side, and cast a trench around thee” — the very thing our Lord foretold of Jerusalem’s siege, that her enemies should cast a trench (a rampart) about her (Luke 19:43). They mark Ellicott’s reference to the sieges of the last fenced cities by Nebuchadnezzar (Jeremiah 34:7) and the Roman sieges. They observe the lesson the witnesses draw: that the strongest defences cannot save a people whom God has given up; the walls they trusted fell, that they might learn their trust was vain. They note that God can bring down the highest fortress. The verse thus foretells the siege — the horror within the besieged city following in the next verses.
53:
On Deuteronomy 28:53 — “And thou shalt eat the fruit of thine own body, the flesh of thy sons and of thy daughters… in the siege, and in the straitness” — the witnesses mark the horror of siege-cannibalism.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: confirmed in the siege of Samaria (2 Kings 6:28) and of Jerusalem by Nebuchadnezzar (Lamentations 4:10). The Reformed Calvin: a portent detestable and more than tragical, that parents should eat their own offspring, whom nature so teaches them to love.

The verse foretells the unspeakable. In the siege they eat their own children. Famine drives nature to its most dreadful crime.

So the witnesses see the curse reach its most dreadful height: that in the extremity of the siege, parents should eat their own children. The witnesses note the horror: “thou shalt eat the fruit of thine own body, the flesh of thy sons and of thy daughters” — Gill marks that nothing can be more shocking or unnatural; the famine of the siege should drive the people to the most monstrous of crimes, the devouring of their own offspring. They mark the cause: “in the siege, and in the straitness, wherewith thine enemies shall distress thee” — the besieged city, cut off from all food, should be reduced to such extremity of hunger that the unspeakable was done. They observe Calvin’s word of horror: that this is one of those portents, an act of ferocity detestable and more than tragical, that fathers and mothers should eat their own children, whom nature so strongly teaches them to love; the very bond of nature broken by the rage of famine. They observe the dreadful fulfilments the witnesses point to: that this was confirmed to the letter in the siege of Samaria by the Syrians, where two women agreed to boil and eat their sons (2 Kings 6:28–29); in the siege of Jerusalem by Nebuchadnezzar, where the pitiful women sod their own children (Lamentations 4:10); and again (the next verses show) in the Roman siege. They note that no curse could be more terrible, nor any prophecy more exactly and awfully fulfilled. They observe that sin, fully ripened in judgment, drives men below the beasts. They note that the breaking of God’s law issues in the breaking of nature itself. The verse thus foretells the unspeakable — the tender man’s cruelty following in the next verses.
54:
On Deuteronomy 28:54 — “So that the man that is tender among you, and very delicate, his eye shall be evil toward his brother, and toward the wife of his bosom… the remnant of his children” — the witnesses mark the gentlest man made cruel by famine.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: the effeminate, tender man, his eye evil toward brother, wife, and children. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: “evil,” unkind, envious, covetous to keep these dreadful morsels to himself, grudging that his dearest relations should share.

The verse shows the gentlest hardened. The tender man grudges his own flesh to his brother and wife. Famine turns love to cruelty.

So the witnesses see famine turn even the gentlest and most tender-hearted man into a creature of grudging cruelty toward his own family. The witnesses note the man described: “the man that is tender among you, and very delicate” — Gill and Keil mark him as one bred softly and used to delicate living, naturally gentle and kind; not a hard or rustic man, but one of refined and tender nature. They mark the dreadful change in him: “his eye shall be evil toward his brother, and toward the wife of his bosom, and… the remnant of his children” — Wesley explains “evil” here as unkind, envious, and covetous: that this tender man, in the extremity of the famine, should grudge to share even the horrible food (the flesh of his own children) with his own brother, his beloved wife, and his remaining children; he should look with a grudging, envious eye on his dearest relations, unwilling to give them any part. They observe the depth of the horror the witnesses feel: that famine should so unman the gentlest, that he who by nature would have given his life for his family should now grudge them a morsel of the most dreadful food; the tenderest affections turned to a fierce and miserly cruelty. They note the truth this discloses: that extreme suffering can corrupt the best of natures, and that there is no depth of selfishness to which the rage of hunger may not drive even a loving man, when God gives a people over to such judgment. They observe that the curse strikes not only the body but the heart, hardening love into cruelty. They note that the breaking of the covenant unmakes the very bonds of the family. The verse thus shows the gentlest hardened — the like of the tender woman following in the next verses.
55:
On Deuteronomy 28:55 — “So that he will not give to any of them of the flesh of his children whom he shall eat: because he hath nothing left him in the siege” — the witnesses mark the hoarding of the unspeakable food.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: he will not give of the flesh of his children, because nothing is left him in the siege. Charles Ellicott: a complication of horrors — they eat their children and refuse to share even this.

The verse deepens the horror. He shares with none the flesh of his children he eats. The straitness piles dread on dread.

So the witnesses see the horror deepened, the tender man hoarding to himself even the dreadful flesh of his own children. The witnesses note the refusal to share: “he will not give to any of them of the flesh of his children whom he shall eat” — the man who has been driven to eat his own child should yet, in his extremity, refuse to give any part of that unspeakable food even to his own brother, wife, or remaining children; his selfishness so total that he would keep the horror to himself. They mark the cause: “because he hath nothing left him in the siege, and in the straitness” — the famine should be so utter, nothing else left in the besieged city, that even this most dreadful food was clung to and grudged. They observe Ellicott’s summing of it: that here is a complication of horrors — that they should eat their own children, and then refuse to share even this with those that are left; the dreadfulness piled on dreadfulness, each detail worse than the last. They note the depth to which the witnesses see human nature sunk by such judgment: that the famine should not only drive men to the unnatural crime, but strip from them the last shred of natural affection, so that the father grudges his own family a part of his own murdered child; love, pity, and shame all consumed by hunger. They observe that this is the very bottom of the curse, the utter ruin of nature and of the family. They note that sin, fully judged, leaves man a monster to his own. They observe that no fulfilment of prophecy is more terrible than this, fulfilled in the sieges of Jerusalem. The verse thus deepens the horror — the like of the tender woman following in the next verses.
56:
On Deuteronomy 28:56 — “The tender and delicate woman among you… her eye shall be evil toward the husband of her bosom, and toward her son, and toward her daughter” — the witnesses mark the gentlest woman made cruel.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: literally fulfilled when Jerusalem was besieged by the Romans — a noble woman, Mary, driven to distraction by famine, boiled and ate her own child. Charles Ellicott: fulfilled to the letter in Mary of Beth-ezob, told by Josephus. The Reformed Gill: instanced because of her sex, more pitiful, especially one bred genteelly. Wesley: “evil,” unmerciful, designing their destruction for her food.

The verse shows the tender woman hardened. The delicate woman grudges her husband and children. Even a mother is made cruel by famine.

So the witnesses see the horror extended to the tender woman, the mother, made cruel toward her own husband and children by the famine. The witnesses note the woman described: “the tender and delicate woman among you, which would not adventure to set the sole of her foot upon the ground for delicateness” — Gill marks that she is instanced because of her sex, naturally more pitiful and compassionate, and especially one bred so genteelly and softly that she would scarcely set her foot on the ground; the gentlest and most delicate of women. They mark the dreadful change: “her eye shall be evil toward the husband of her bosom, and toward her son, and toward her daughter” — Wesley explains “evil” as unmerciful: that this tender mother should, in the famine, design even the destruction of her own husband and children for her food, grudging them and looking on them with a cruel eye. They observe the awful fulfilment the witnesses point to: that this was literally and exactly fulfilled when Jerusalem was besieged by the Romans, in the case of Mary of Beth-ezob, a woman of noble family, who, driven to distraction by famine, boiled and ate her own infant child — a story told, Clarke and Ellicott note, with horrible minuteness by Josephus, and after him by Eusebius. They observe the depth of the curse the witnesses see: that the famine should so corrupt even a mother’s love — the strongest of natural affections — that she should devour her own child; nothing in nature was left untouched by the judgment. They note that the prophecy, written near a thousand years before, was fulfilled to the letter in the Roman siege. They observe that the breaking of the covenant brought a curse that broke the mother’s heart of pity. The verse thus shows the tender woman hardened — her secret eating of her children following in the next verse.
57:
On Deuteronomy 28:57 — “And toward her young one… and toward her children which she shall bear: for she shall eat them for want of all things secretly in the siege” — the witnesses mark the secret eating of her own children.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: she shall eat them for want of all things secretly in the siege. Adam Clarke and Wesley: “her young one” (the afterbirth), loathsome to behold, now pleasant to eat, and with it the child wrapped in it. The Reformed Gill: her secundine, the afterbirth, as the Targum and Aben Ezra.

The verse completes the horror. In secret she eats her own newborn and children for want. The siege brings the uttermost dread.

So the witnesses see the horror brought to its uttermost: the delicate woman secretly devouring her own newborn and her children for want of all things. The witnesses note the dreadful objects of her hunger: “her young one that cometh out from between her feet, and… her children which she shall bear” — Gill, Clarke, and Wesley explain “her young one” as the secundine or afterbirth, a thing loathsome even to look upon, which yet in the famine she should be driven to eat; and with it, Wesley adds, the newborn child wrapped in it, and her other children too. They mark the manner: “she shall eat them for want of all things secretly in the siege” — she should do it in secret, both from the shame of so dreadful a deed and from the fear that others, as starved as she, should snatch the food from her; the want should be so total that even this was clung to and hidden. They observe the depth of the curse the witnesses see: that the woman whose nature was all tenderness should be reduced to eat what is most loathsome and most dear — the afterbirth, the newborn, the children of her own womb; the famine breaking through every barrier of nature, decency, and love. They note that this completes the catalogue of siege-horrors begun above, the most terrible curse in all the Scripture, and the most exactly fulfilled in the sieges of Jerusalem. They observe that the witnesses dwell on these things not for horror’s sake, but that the dreadfulness of forsaking God might be felt; the curse is as great as the covenant was gracious. They note that sin, ripened in judgment, brings a misery beyond all imagining. The verse thus completes the horror — the charge to keep the law following in the next verse.
58:
On Deuteronomy 28:58 — “If thou wilt not observe to do all the words of this law… that thou mayest fear this glorious and fearful name, THE LORD THY GOD” — the witnesses mark the condition and the fear of God’s name.

The Reformed Gill: the words of this law written in this book of Deuteronomy, to be observed and done. Calvin: even believers, though willing, are hindered by the flesh; therefore care is taken to keep them in fear.

The verse states the condition. If they keep not the law, to fear God’s glorious name, the plagues follow. Reverence for God is the thing required.

So the witnesses see the long catalogue of curses hung again upon the condition of disobedience, and especially of failing to fear God’s name. The witnesses note the condition: “if thou wilt not observe to do all the words of this law that are written in this book” — Gill observes these are the words of the law written in the book of Deuteronomy, the repetition and addition of the commandments; the curses come upon the failure to observe and do them all. They mark the end set forth: “that thou mayest fear this glorious and fearful name, THE LORD THY GOD” — the keeping of the law was to issue in, and to express, the fear of God’s great and dreadful name; the reverence of God is the root of all obedience, and the want of it the root of all the disobedience that brings the curse. They observe the weight the witnesses see in the naming of God: “this glorious and fearful name” — the name of the LORD, in which all his majesty and holiness are summed, is glorious to be adored and fearful to be dreaded; to fear that name is the beginning of wisdom (Proverbs 9:10), and to despise it the height of folly. They note Calvin’s tender observation: that even true believers, though willing and earnest to obey, are still hindered by the infirmity of their flesh from fulfilling their duty; and so God in mercy adds these fearful sanctions, to keep even his own people in awe and to spur them on, lest they grow careless. They observe that the fear of God is the safeguard of obedience. They note that to lose the fear of God’s name is to open the door to all the curse. The verse thus states the condition — the wonderful plagues following in the next verses.
59:
On Deuteronomy 28:59 — “Then the LORD will make thy plagues wonderful, and the plagues of thy seed, even great plagues, and of long continuance, and sore sicknesses, and of long continuance” — the witnesses mark the wonderful and lasting plagues.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: the LORD would make their strokes wonderful, great and long-continued plagues and sicknesses. The Reformed Gill: wonderful, that is, visible, remarkable, astonishing to all who see them.

The verse threatens lasting plagues. God will make their plagues wonderful, great, and long. The judgments are remarkable and enduring.

So the witnesses see the plagues threatened as wonderful, great, and of long continuance, upon the people and their seed. The witnesses note the strangeness of the strokes: “the LORD will make thy plagues wonderful” — Gill explains “wonderful” as visible, remarkable, and distinguishable, astonishing to all who saw them; the judgments should be no ordinary calamities, but such as marked the hand of God, extraordinary in their kind and degree, that men should wonder at them. They mark the extent and the duration: “great plagues, and of long continuance, and sore sicknesses, and of long continuance” — the strokes should be great in their severity and long in their endurance, not passing quickly but cleaving to the people; and reaching to their seed after them, the children sharing the plagues of the fathers. They observe the dread the witnesses see in long-continued judgment: that a great affliction that swiftly passes may be borne, but one that is both great and lasting, that wears on without relief, is among the heaviest of strokes; God threatens not a momentary blow but an enduring scourge. They note the contrast with the blessing the witnesses recall: that God had promised, in obedience, to take all sickness from them and to keep them from the diseases of Egypt (Exodus 15:26); in disobedience, he would make their plagues wonderful and long. They observe that the hand of God, when it smites in judgment, can lay on strokes both extraordinary and abiding. They note that the wonder of the plagues was itself a testimony to the wonder of the sin. The verse thus threatens the lasting plagues — the diseases of Egypt following in the next verses.
60:
On Deuteronomy 28:60 — “Moreover he will bring upon thee all the diseases of Egypt, which thou wast afraid of; and they shall cleave unto thee” — the witnesses mark the diseases of Egypt brought back.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: all the diseases of Egypt, which they dreaded, would cleave to them. Charles Ellicott: contrast Exodus 15:26 — “I will put none of these diseases upon thee.” The Reformed Gill: all the diseases brought on the Egyptians, or peculiar to them.

The verse brings Egypt’s plagues. The diseases of Egypt they feared shall cleave to them. The plagues of their old oppressors return on them.

So the witnesses see the plagues of Egypt, which the people dreaded, brought back upon them and made to cleave to them. The witnesses note the diseases: “all the diseases of Egypt, which thou wast afraid of” — Gill takes these as either the diseases God had brought on the Egyptians in judgment (the plagues), or the diseases peculiar to and common in the land of Egypt; the maladies of that country, which Israel had seen and feared. They mark the bitter irony Ellicott draws out: that this is the exact reversal of the promise of Exodus 15:26, where God had said, “If thou wilt diligently hearken to my voice… I will put none of these diseases of Egypt upon thee, which thou knowest; for I am the LORD that healeth thee”; the very diseases God had pledged, in obedience, to keep from them, he would, in disobedience, bring upon them. They observe the clinging of the plagues: “they shall cleave unto thee” — the diseases should not pass, but fasten upon them and stay, an abiding affliction; the curse holding fast where it lighted. They note the dread the witnesses feel: that the people should be afflicted with the very plagues they had once seen fall on their oppressors and had been thankful to escape; the judgments of Egypt, from which they were redeemed, returning upon them for their unfaithfulness. They observe the principle the witnesses see: that the mercies God gives in obedience he reverses in rebellion; the healing God turned to plague, the redemption to a return of the curse. They note that to forsake the LORD who heals is to fall back under the diseases he removed. The verse thus brings Egypt’s plagues — every other sickness following in the next verse.
61:
On Deuteronomy 28:61 — “Also every sickness, and every plague, which is not written in the book of this law, them will the LORD bring upon thee, until thou be destroyed” — the witnesses mark the unnamed plagues.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: every sickness and plague (makkah, a smiting) not written — well might the Apostle say, “It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God” (Hebrews 10:31). The Reformed Gill: whatever sickness could be thought of or named. Calvin: the plague has a wider meaning, comprehending all the curses.

The verse adds the unnamed. Every sickness not written, God will bring, till destruction. No plague is excepted.

So the witnesses see the catalogue of plagues opened beyond all naming: every sickness, even those not written, God will bring. The witnesses note the comprehensiveness: “every sickness, and every plague, which is not written in the book of this law” — Gill observes that whatever sickness or disease could be thought of or named, even those not mentioned or threatened in the law, God would bring upon them; the written curses were not the whole, but a sample; God had in store more than the book could contain. They mark Calvin’s note that the word “plague” (or smiting) has here a wide meaning, comprehending all the curses; the sickness named first as a part, and then the plague following, taking in the whole. They observe Ellicott’s solemn application: that in the face of such a threat — of every conceivable and inconceivable plague brought by God himself — well might the Apostle write, “It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God” (Hebrews 10:31); the God whose hand can lay on plagues without number is dreadful to the disobedient. They note the dread the witnesses feel in the open-endedness of the curse: that the people could not, by escaping the named plagues, escape the wrath; God had ways of judgment beyond their reckoning, and would not be at a loss for means to destroy. They observe the end: “until thou be destroyed” — the plagues should not cease till the people were brought low. They note that the resources of God’s wrath are as inexhaustible as the riches of his mercy. They observe that to fall under his hand in judgment is the most fearful of all things. The verse thus adds the unnamed plagues — the people left few in number following in the next verse.
62:
On Deuteronomy 28:62 — “And ye shall be left few in number, whereas ye were as the stars of heaven for multitude; because thou wouldest not obey the voice of the LORD thy God” — the witnesses mark the people reduced to a handful.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: Israel would be almost annihilated thereby. The Reformed Gill: very few left in Judea by Nebuchadnezzar’s general, of the poorer sort. Calvin: since it was promised to Abraham that his seed should be as the stars, it was a signal token of wrath that they should be reduced to a handful.

The verse reduces the multitude. They are left few, who were as the stars. The promise of multitude is reversed in judgment.

So the witnesses see the great multitude of Israel reduced, in judgment, to a small remnant. The witnesses note the reduction: “ye shall be left few in number” — Gill observes that very few were left in the land of Judea by Nebuchadnezzar’s general when Jerusalem was taken, and those of the poorer sort, left for vinedressers and husbandmen; Keil marks that Israel would be almost annihilated by the plagues and the sword. They mark the bitter contrast: “whereas ye were as the stars of heaven for multitude” — Calvin draws out the force of it: since it had been promised to Abraham that his seed should be like the stars of heaven for multitude (Genesis 15:5), it was a signal and dreadful token of God’s wrath that his posterity should be reduced to a mere handful of people; the very promise of increase reversed, the multitude become a few. They observe the cause restated: “because thou wouldest not obey the voice of the LORD thy God” — all this came of their disobedience; the dwindling of the nation the just fruit of their refusing to hear God. They note the lesson the witnesses see: that the blessings God gives, even those founded on his promise to the fathers, are enjoyed only in the way of obedience; the people who would not obey forfeited, for a time, the increase that was their heritage. They observe that God can as easily diminish as multiply, and that the same hand that made them as the stars could leave them a handful. They note that the curse reached even to the number of the people. The verse thus reduces the multitude — God’s rejoicing to destroy following in the next verse.
63:
On Deuteronomy 28:63 — “As the LORD rejoiced over you to do you good… so the LORD will rejoice over you to destroy you, and to bring you to nought” — the witnesses mark God rejoicing in judgment as in mercy.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: as the LORD rejoiced to do them good, so he would rejoice to destroy and pluck them off the land. The Reformed Calvin: the wonderful love of God set forth, that he had rejoiced in blessing them; their depravity all the more base. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: he delights not in the death of a sinner in itself, yet delights in glorifying his justice.

The verse declares God’s pleasure in judgment. As he rejoiced to bless, so he will rejoice to destroy. His justice is glorified in their ruin.

So the witnesses see the dreadful declaration that God would rejoice in destroying the disobedient as he had rejoiced in blessing them. The witnesses note the parallel: “as the LORD rejoiced over you to do you good… so the LORD will rejoice over you to destroy you” — the same God who had taken delight and pleasure in doing them good, multiplying and blessing them, would take a kind of pleasure in their ruin when they forsook him; the contrast measures the change. They mark Calvin’s reflection on the love thus implied: that the wonderful and inestimable love of God toward his people is here set forth, in that he had rejoiced in heaping blessings upon them; and that this made their depravity all the more base and intolerable, that they should so requite a God who had so delighted in their good. They observe the careful guarding of the words the witnesses give, lest God be thought cruel. Wesley draws the needful distinction: that God does not delight in the death of a sinner in itself (Ezekiel 33:11); but he does delight in glorifying his justice, and so, when his patience is despised and his mercy refused, he takes a holy satisfaction in executing the judgment that vindicates his righteousness. They note the solemn truth the witnesses see: that the God who is slow to anger and rich in mercy will yet, when sin is ripe and grace is spurned, magnify his justice in the destruction of the impenitent, and that with a kind of pleasure, as the doing of a right and necessary thing. They observe the warning: that to presume on God’s love is to forget that the same God is just. They note that mercy refused turns to a consuming judgment. The verse thus declares God’s pleasure in judgment — the scattering following in the next verse.
64:
On Deuteronomy 28:64 — “And the LORD shall scatter thee among all people, from the one end of the earth even unto the other; and there thou shalt serve other gods, which neither thou nor thy fathers have known, even wood and stone” — the witnesses mark the scattering among all nations.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: how literally fulfilled! The Jews are scattered over every nation under heaven. Charles Ellicott: fulfilled literally in this last dispersion. The Reformed Gill: their present dispersion, in all parts of the world. Calvin: banishment was far more painful to Israel than to other nations, affection for country being natural.

The verse foretells the dispersion. They are scattered to the ends of the earth, to serve idols. The exile reaches to all nations.

So the witnesses see the curse reach its widest sweep: the scattering of the people among all the nations of the earth. The witnesses note the dispersion: “the LORD shall scatter thee among all people, from the one end of the earth even unto the other” — Gill takes this of their present dispersion, being, more or fewer, in all parts of the world, east, west, north, and south; Clarke marvels how literally it has been fulfilled, the Jews scattered over every nation under heaven; Ellicott marks it fulfilled to the letter in this last and lasting dispersion under the Romans. They mark the bitterness of it for Israel especially, which Calvin draws out: that banishment was far more painful to the people of Israel than to other nations, since affection for one’s country is natural to all, and theirs was the land of promise, the holy land, from which to be cast out was a peculiar grief. They observe the spiritual peril of the scattering: “there thou shalt serve other gods… wood and stone” — in their dispersion among idolatrous nations, they should be drawn to serve false gods; Ellicott notes that, though we do not know of Israel falling into actual idolatry in the dispersion (save in Egypt, Jeremiah 44:8), yet the danger and the threat stood, the scattered people exposed to the gods of the nations. They observe the fulfilment the witnesses see as a standing wonder: the Jew in every land, scattered to the ends of the earth, the prophecy written in their very condition. They note that the people who would not serve God in their own land were scattered among the nations. The verse thus foretells the dispersion — the restless terror of it following in the next verses.
65:
On Deuteronomy 28:65 — “And among these nations shalt thou find no ease, neither shall the sole of thy foot have rest: but the LORD shall give thee there a trembling heart, and failing of eyes, and sorrow of mind” — the witnesses mark the restless terror of the dispersed.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: a trembling heart and failing eyes — their state of continual insecurity, proscribed, uncertain of toleration. Charles Ellicott: the repeated persecutions of the Jews among the most fearful phenomena of history. The Reformed Gill: no quiet settlement, moved from place to place by cruel edicts and fines. Wesley: tossed about, sold from person to person, Cain-like wandering.

The verse marks their unrest. No ease, no rest, but a trembling heart and failing eyes. The dispersed live in perpetual fear.

So the witnesses see the dispersed people given over to a restless, trembling misery among the nations. The witnesses note the want of rest: “among these nations shalt thou find no ease, neither shall the sole of thy foot have rest” — Gill observes they should have no quiet settlement nor certain dwelling, being driven from place to place by cruel edicts, heavy fines, and persecutions; Wesley adds that they should be tossed about, sold from person to person, and, Cain-like, wander the earth without a resting-place.
