They mark the inward terror: “the LORD shall give thee there a trembling heart, and failing of eyes, and sorrow of mind” — Clarke explains the trembling heart as their state of continual insecurity, everywhere proscribed and, even under the mildest governments, uncertain of toleration; the failing of eyes the looking and longing for a relief that never surely came; the sorrow of mind the abiding grief and dread of so unsettled a life. They observe the dreadful and lasting fulfilment the witnesses point to: that the repeated persecutions of the Jews in the ages of their dispersion — the expulsions, massacres, and proscriptions — are, as Ellicott says, among the most fearful and wonderful phenomena of all history, the very picture this verse drew near a thousand years before. They note that the curse reached not only their outward state but their inmost peace, robbing them of all rest of body and of mind. They observe that to forsake God, the rest of the soul, is to find no rest anywhere. They note that the trembling heart is the mark of those who have lost their God. The verse thus marks their unrest — the doubt of life following in the next verse.
66:
On Deuteronomy 28:66 — “And thy life shall hang in doubt before thee; and thou shalt fear day and night, and shalt have none assurance of thy life” — the witnesses mark the constant fear of death.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: thy life will hang before thee, like some valued object hung up, threatened to be torn from thee. Charles Ellicott: “perhaps I shall die today by the sword” (Rashi). The Reformed Gill: whether life shall be spared by the enemy or not. Wesley: in the hands of enemies who have power and will to destroy, and the terrors of a guilty conscience.

The verse marks the dread of death. Their life hangs in doubt; they fear day and night. They have no certainty even of living.

So the witnesses see the dispersed and afflicted people kept in a perpetual dread of death, their very life hanging in doubt. The witnesses note the figure: “thy life shall hang in doubt before thee” — Keil draws out the image, that their life would be like some valued object hung up before their eyes, always in danger of being torn down and snatched away; the life they prized held by a thread, ever in peril. They mark the unceasing fear: “thou shalt fear day and night, and shalt have none assurance of thy life” — Gill observes they should not know from one moment to the next whether their life would be spared by the enemy; Rashi’s gloss, which Ellicott cites, catches it: “perhaps I shall die today by the sword that cometh upon me.” They observe Wesley’s double cause of this terror: that it sprang both from their outward state, being in the hands of enemies who had the power and wanted not the will to destroy them; and from their inward state, the terrors of their own mind and the guilt of conscience making them their own tormentors. They note the depth of the curse the witnesses see: that to have no assurance of one’s very life, to fear death every hour of day and night, is a misery worse than death itself, a living dread that never lets go. They observe that the people who would not trust God for their life lost all assurance of it; the fear of death, from which God’s people are freed (Hebrews 2:15), held them in lifelong bondage. They note that a guilty conscience makes its own hell of fear. The verse thus marks the dread of death — the longing of morning and evening following in the next verse.
67:
On Deuteronomy 28:67 — “In the morning thou shalt say, Would God it were even! and at even thou shalt say, Would God it were morning! for the fear of thine heart… and for the sight of thine eyes” — the witnesses mark the unrelieved misery of fear.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: in the morning they would long for evening, and at evening for morning, for the fear of their heart and the sight of their eyes. Charles Ellicott: the Talmud expounds it of the constant increase of trouble — today the trouble is more terrible than yesterday. The Reformed Gill: wishing they might get through the day, fearing their life taken before night.

The verse shows the misery without relief. Morning they wish for evening, evening for morning. No hour brings ease.

So the witnesses see the misery so unrelieved that the sufferers long, at every hour, for the hour to pass, finding ease in none. The witnesses note the restless longing: “in the morning thou shalt say, Would God it were even! and at even thou shalt say, Would God it were morning!” — Gill explains that in the morning they should wish the day already over, fearing their life would be taken or some fresh calamity fall before night; and at evening they should wish it were morning again, the terrors of the night being worse than the day; no hour brought rest, but each was dreaded, and each longed to be past. They mark the cause: “for the fear of thine heart wherewith thou shalt fear, and for the sight of thine eyes which thou shalt see” — the inward dread of their heart and the outward horrors their eyes beheld together made every hour a torment, so that they could only wish the present moment gone. They observe the dreadful sense the witnesses (with the Talmud, cited by Ellicott) draw out: that the trouble was ever increasing, so that yesterday’s evening was longed for this morning, but today’s trouble is more terrible still, and every hour adds to the curse; there was no point of rest, only a worsening misery. They note the depth of wretchedness the witnesses see: that to find no ease at any hour, to long always for the hour to pass and dread the next, is the very picture of a life forsaken of all comfort. They observe that those who forsook the God of all comfort were left without comfort day or night. They note that such restlessness is the fruit of a heart without God. The verse thus shows the unrelieved misery — the return to Egypt in ships following in the last verse.
68:
On Deuteronomy 28:68 — “And the LORD shall bring thee into Egypt again with ships… and there ye shall be sold unto your enemies for bondmen and bondwomen, and no man shall buy you” — the witnesses mark the final return to bondage.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: into another state of slavery like Egypt; exposed to sale, but none to buy, the supply so great. Charles Ellicott: Josephus says this was done with many of the Jews by Titus. The Reformed Gill and Calvin: strictly and literally brought into Egypt again. Wesley: literally fulfilled under Titus, when multitudes were carried thither in ships and sold for slaves.

The verse closes the curses. They are carried back to Egypt in ships, sold as slaves, none buying. The redemption is utterly reversed.

So the witnesses see the catalogue of curses close with the bitterest reversal of all: the people carried back to Egypt and sold as slaves, with none to buy. The witnesses note the return: “the LORD shall bring thee into Egypt again with ships” — Gill, Calvin, and Wesley take this strictly and literally, that the people should be carried back to Egypt, the house of their old bondage; and they mark the pointed contrast in “with ships,” that whereas at the Exodus they went out of Egypt by land, through the divided sea, in triumph, they should now be brought back over the sea in ships, as captives — the very way of their redemption reversed. They observe the bondage: “there ye shall be sold unto your enemies for bondmen and bondwomen” — they should be sold into slavery, the very thing from which God had redeemed their fathers; the redeemed people made slaves again. They mark the crowning misery: “and no man shall buy you” — Clarke explains that they should be exposed for sale, but the supply of captives so great, and they so despised, that none would even buy them; sold slaves whom no one valued enough to purchase, the lowest depth of contempt and worthlessness. They observe the dreadful fulfilment the witnesses point to: that Josephus records this was done with multitudes of the Jews by Titus after the fall of Jerusalem, carried into Egypt in ships and sold for slaves, and so cheap and so many that the market was glutted and none would buy. They note that the chapter, which began with the blessing of obedience, ends in this uttermost curse, the redemption undone; and that the whole pointed beyond itself to the curse of the law from which Christ alone redeems (Galatians 3:13). They observe that to forsake the God who redeemed them was to fall back, worthless, into the bondage he had broken. The verse thus closes the curses — the dread end of forsaking the covenant.
